skill.name: llm-app-patterns
skill.description: Production patterns for designing LLM applications, including RAG pipelines, agents, prompt workflows, LLMOps, caching, retries, and fallback strategies.
skill.summary: Design practical LLM applications with RAG, agent, prompt, and LLMOps patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, sensitive file, database, and weak crypto patterns. Manual review found those signals are mostly Markdown fences, diagrams, identifiers, and resource links, not active malicious code. The remaining concern is documentation-level risk from examples that omit tool-execution guardrails and log prompt data.

medium_findings.title: Agent Tool Execution Examples Need Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The ReAct and function-calling examples execute parsed model actions or model-selected tool calls through placeholder methods. The skill does not run this code, but copied implementations should add tool allowlists, schema validation, permission checks, and approval gates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly pass LLM-derived actions into execution helpers, but the helpers are placeholders. This is a real implementation risk if copied, not evidence of malicious behavior in the skill.

medium_findings.title: Prompt Logging Example May Expose Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The observability example logs prompt excerpts and user identifiers. This can expose confidential user data if implemented without redaction, access controls, and retention limits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logged fields include prompt content and user_id, which are common sources of sensitive data. The code is instructional, so the risk depends on how a user implements it.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick detections are caused by Markdown code fences and diagrams. No executable Ruby backtick command or shell invocation was found in the reviewed skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are fenced Markdown blocks containing diagrams or Python examples. There is no evidence of a Ruby shell execution path.

low_findings.title: Static Network Findings Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are reference links to public project documentation. The request-related line is a method name in a logging example, not an HTTP client call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in prose resource links, and log_request is a local method name. No code sends data to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive File, Database, and Crypto Findings Are Identifier Matches
low_findings.description: The key, database, and weak-crypto detections are variable names or benign examples. The cache key example uses SHA-256 for deterministic cache keys, not weak cryptography or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines show a variable named kwargs.keys, a placeholder db object, and hashlib.sha256. No certificate file, SQLite file, MD5, SHA-1, or credential material was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: LLM-Selected Tool Calls Without Explicit Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show model-selected tools being executed by helper methods. Production systems should validate tool names, arguments, permissions, and side effects before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execution path is visible in the examples, but the called helper implementations are not present. This supports a cautionary finding rather than a malicious-code finding.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt and User Identifier Logging
dangerous_patterns.description: The logging pattern records prompt text and user identifiers. This should be paired with redaction, consent, access controls, and retention limits.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The logged fields are explicit in the example. The risk is contextual because the skill is documentation and does not run the logger itself.

content.value_statement: Teams need clear patterns for reliable LLM applications. This skill organizes RAG, agent, prompt, observability, and production patterns into practical design guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains RAG ingestion, retrieval, and context generation patterns.
Compares vector database and embedding model options for common workloads.
Describes ReAct, function calling, plan-execute, and multi-agent architectures.
Shows prompt template, prompt registry, A/B testing, and prompt chaining patterns.
Defines LLMOps metrics, logging, tracing, evaluation, caching, retry, and fallback patterns.
Provides an architecture decision matrix for selecting common LLM patterns.
limitations: It provides design guidance and snippets, not a complete runnable framework.
Provider setup, credentials, and deployment configuration must be added separately.
Example code needs security hardening before production use.
It does not benchmark model quality or cost in your environment.

use_cases.title: Design a RAG Search Feature
use_cases.description: Plan document ingestion, chunking, embeddings, retrieval, and answer generation for a knowledge product.

use_cases.title: Select an Agent Architecture
use_cases.description: Compare ReAct, function calling, plan-execute, and multi-agent options for a new AI workflow.

use_cases.title: Add LLMOps Controls
use_cases.description: Define metrics, tracing, evaluations, caching, retries, and fallback behavior for production operations.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Starting Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-app-patterns to recommend a simple architecture for this LLM feature: [describe feature]. Explain the main tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a RAG Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-app-patterns to design a RAG pipeline for [data type]. Include chunking, retrieval, generation, and evaluation choices.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Agent Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-app-patterns to compare ReAct, function calling, plan-execute, and multi-agent designs for [workflow]. Recommend one option.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Production Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use llm-app-patterns to review this LLM application design: [paste design]. Identify reliability, observability, cost, and security gaps.

output_examples.input: A team wants internal document question answering for support staff.
output_examples.output: Recommended pattern: Simple RAG with document-aware chunking, hybrid retrieval, grounded answer generation, citations, and offline evaluation cases.

output_examples.input: A product lead needs an agent for multi-step account analysis.
output_examples.output: Recommended pattern: Function calling for structured tools first. Add plan-execute only if tasks require explicit multi-step planning.

output_examples.input: A platform team needs production readiness checks for LLM calls.
output_examples.output: Suggested controls: latency metrics, token cost tracking, prompt redaction, evaluation sets, deterministic caching, retry backoff, and model fallback.

best_practices: Start with the simplest pattern that satisfies the user workflow.
Add evaluations, tracing, and cost metrics before broad production rollout.
Harden copied examples with validation, redaction, permissions, and human approval where needed.
anti_patterns: Do not use agents when a deterministic RAG or function call flow is enough.
Do not log full prompts, responses, or user identifiers without privacy controls.
Do not copy example tool execution logic into production without validation and authorization.

faq.question: Is this a runnable LLM framework?
faq.answer: No. It is a pattern guide with examples that you adapt to your stack.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is tool-agnostic and is marked as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include RAG guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers ingestion, chunking, embeddings, vector stores, retrieval, and grounded generation.

faq.question: Does it cover AI agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains ReAct, function calling, plan-execute, and multi-agent collaboration patterns.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review tool permissions, prompt privacy, logging, retries, caching, fallbacks, and evaluation coverage.

faq.question: Does it choose a model provider for me?
faq.answer: No. It compares patterns and leaves provider selection to your application requirements.
